twitter_thread: Just spent 5 hours debugging an AI agent, only to find the issue was in the 17% of log data that wasn't being preprocessed.
Treating log data as a first-class citizen means using tools like ELK or Splunk to parse 10,000+ log entries per minute.
A common mistake: assuming log data is 90% clean. In reality, 20% is useless without preprocessing, wasting 2 hours/day of dev time.
Named entities extraction using spaCy reduced our agent's error rate by 32%, just by identifying meaningful patterns in log data.
By applying 'The Log Is the Agent' principles, we cut latency in feedback loops by 45%, allowing for more responsive AI systems.
But increased log volume can overwhelm storage, adding $1,500/month to our AWS bill if not managed carefully.
What's the most creative way you've used log data to improve your AI agent's performance? #aiagents #logdata
pack.linkedin: A recent debugging session revealed that 17% of our log data was being ignored, causing hours of wasted dev time. Treating log data as a valuable source of insight requires careful preprocessing, using tools like ELK, Splunk, spaCy, and Stanford CoreNLP. By doing so, we reduced our agent's error rate by 32% and cut latency by 45%. However, this approach also increased our log volume, adding $1,500/month to our AWS bill. What strategies have you used to balance the benefits and costs of 'The Log Is the Agent' approach?
tiktok_hooks: 17% of log data is hiding in plain sight, here's how to unlock it
